conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do! I have a child, Alex, who's 8 years old and lives with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask a few more questions.

Are you or your partner responsible for Alex? This means you're looking after them and contributing to the costs of raising them (like food, clothing, and housing).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm responsible for Alex. I look after them and cover all their costs - food, clothing, housing, everything they need.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. Next question:

Do you normally live in the UK? (This includes England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. I have a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Is Alex currently in hospital or in care (such as local authority care)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in care with the local authority for a couple of months, but they come home to stay with me regularly - they spend quite a bit of time at home each week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that information. This is an important detail for your eligibility.

When Alex is in local authority care, are you still contributing to the costs of looking after them during that time? And approximately how many days per week does Alex typically spend at home with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm still contributing to their costs while they're in care. Alex typically comes home and spends time with me - I'd say they're with me for more than 24 hours each week, usually staying over on weekends and some evenings.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. Based on what you've told me, let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

According to the government guidance, when a child is in local authority care, you can still claim Child Benefit if the child spends at least one night a week at home with you on average, or if you're still contributing at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards the costs of looking after them.

In your case, Alex spends more than 24 hours with you each week (including weekend stays), which means they're spending at least one night a week with you, and you're also contributing to their costs. This means you should be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me provide you with the formal assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news!** You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Even though Alex is in local authority care, you meet the eligibility criteria because:
- Alex spends at least one night per week at home with you on average (you mentioned they're with you for more than 24 hours weekly, including weekend stays)
- You're still contributing to Alex's costs

The special rules for children in care allow you to claim Child Benefit as long as the child spends at least one night a week at home with you, or you contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them. You meet both of these conditions, so you should be able to claim.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 8 years old (under 16, so meets the age requirement). The claimant is responsible for Alex and contributes to all their costs. The claimant lives in the UK. Although Alex is in local authority care, they spend more than 24 hours per week at home with the claimant (including weekend stays), which means they spend at least one night a week at home on average. According to government guidance, you can still get Child Benefit when a child is in care if they spend at least one night a week at home with you on average, or if you contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their costs. The claimant meets both criteria, therefore they are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 8), who is in local authority care but regularly stays with the claimant for more than 24 hours per week. The claimant lives in the UK, is responsible for Alex, and contributes to their costs. Despite Alex being in care, the claimant meets the eligibility criteria because Alex spends at least one night per week at home, which qualifies the claimant for Child Benefit under the special rules for children in care.
